sections.title: System Purpose
sections.content:
Acme API is an internal platform for Acme Corp providing **user management**, **team collaboration**, and **subscription billing**. It is built as a Next.js 15 API with Supabase (PostgreSQL) for persistence, Stripe for payment processing, and Resend for transactional email.

The system serves as the backend for a multi-tenant SaaS product where teams can sign up, invite members, manage roles, and subscribe to paid plans with usage-based limits.
sections.summary: Internal SaaS platform for user management, team collaboration, and Stripe billing

evidence.rationale: Package name and description confirm project purpose

sections.title: Architecture Overview
sections.content:
The application follows a **layered architecture** with clear separation between API routing, business logic, and data access:

```
src/
  api/            → HTTP middleware and route handlers
    middleware/    → Auth, rate limiting, request validation
    routes/        → Auth, teams, billing, health endpoints
  auth/           → Authentication primitives (JWT, sessions, passwords, magic links)
  services/       → Business logic orchestration
  db/             → Data models (Zod schemas) and Supabase queries
  lib/            → Shared utilities (env validation, error types, Supabase clients)
```

**Request flow:** Incoming requests pass through Next.js Edge Middleware (`src/middleware.ts`) which enforces rate limiting and extracts auth context. Route handlers in `src/api/routes/` validate input via Zod schemas, delegate to service functions in `src/services/`, which in turn call query functions in `src/db/queries/`.

**External integrations:** Supabase for PostgreSQL + auth storage, Stripe for subscription billing and webhooks, Resend for transactional email (magic links, invitations, welcome messages).
sections.summary: Layered architecture: API routes → services → DB queries, with Supabase, Stripe, and Resend integrations

evidence.rationale: Edge middleware is the entry point for all API requests
Service layer imports demonstrate the layered dependency chain

sections.title: External Dependencies
sections.content:
**Runtime dependencies:**

| Dependency | Purpose | Critical Path |
|---|---|---|
| `@supabase/supabase-js` + `@supabase/ssr` | PostgreSQL database, auth storage, server-side Supabase client | All data operations |
| `jsonwebtoken` | JWT signing and verification for session tokens | Authentication |
| `bcryptjs` | Password hashing with configurable salt rounds (12) | User registration and login |
| `stripe` | Subscription billing, checkout sessions, webhook processing | Billing flow |
| `resend` | Transactional email delivery (magic links, invitations) | Onboarding and team management |
| `zod` | Runtime schema validation for request bodies and env vars | Request validation |

**Infrastructure requirements:**
- Supabase project (PostgreSQL + Auth) or self-hosted equivalent
- Stripe account with configured products and webhook endpoint
- Resend account for email delivery
- Node.js 22+
sections.summary: Supabase (DB), Stripe (billing), Resend (email), JWT + bcrypt (auth), Zod (validation)

sections.title: Domain Model
sections.content:
The system manages three core entities:

**Users** (`src/db/models/user.ts`): Identified by UUID, with email, name, avatar, and bcrypt-hashed password. Created via sign-up or magic link redemption.

**Teams** (`src/db/models/team.ts`): Multi-tenant containers with a name, unique slug, billing plan (free/pro/enterprise), Stripe customer and subscription IDs, and usage tracking (current count vs. limit). Plan limits are defined in `PLAN_LIMITS` — free: 100 uses / 3 members, pro: 10K uses / 25 members, enterprise: unlimited.

**Invitations** (`src/db/models/invitation.ts`): Team invitations sent via email with a cryptographic token, assigned role (admin or member — never owner), 7-day expiration, and acceptance tracking.

**Team Memberships** join users to teams with roles: `owner`, `admin`, or `member`. Permissions are enforced in the service layer — only owners and admins can update teams, invite members, or remove members.
sections.summary: Three entities: Users, Teams (with billing plans), and Invitations — joined by role-based memberships

evidence.rationale: User model definition and validation schemas
Team model with plan tiers and limits
Invitation model with token and expiration

sections.title: Core System Flows
sections.content:
**Authentication flow (REQ-AUTH-001):**
1. User submits email + password → `signIn()` in auth-service → verifies via bcrypt → signs JWT with 7-day TTL → sets `acme-session` httpOnly cookie
2. Alternative: magic link → `requestMagicLink()` → generates crypto token → sends email via Resend → user clicks link → `redeemMagicLink()` consumes token → creates session

**Team invitation flow (REQ-TEAM-002):**
1. Admin calls `inviteTeamMember()` → validates plan member limit → creates invitation with 7-day expiring token → sends email via Resend
2. Recipient clicks link → `acceptTeamInvitation()` → creates team_membership record → marks invitation accepted

**Billing flow (REQ-BILL-001):**
1. Team initiates checkout → `createCheckoutSession()` → creates/retrieves Stripe customer → creates Checkout Session with plan price → returns redirect URL
2. Stripe fires `checkout.session.completed` webhook → `activateSubscription()` → updates team plan to 'pro' and raises usage limit
3. On cancellation, `customer.subscription.deleted` webhook → `downgradeToPlan('free')` → preserves data but restricts limits (REQ-BILL-003)

**Usage tracking (REQ-BILL-002):**
- `trackUsage()` checks current count vs. limit before incrementing
- Atomic increment via Supabase RPC function `increment_team_usage`
sections.summary: Auth (JWT + magic link), team invitations (email + token), Stripe billing (checkout → webhook → activate), usage tracking

evidence.rationale: Authentication orchestration with sign-in, sign-up, magic link flows

sections.title: Code Structure
sections.content:
**Module breakdown (44 files, ~1,800 LOC):**

| Module | Files | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `src/auth/` | 4 | JWT, password hashing, sessions, magic links |
| `src/api/middleware/` | 3 | Auth extraction, rate limiting, Zod validation |
| `src/api/routes/` | 4 | Route handlers for auth, teams, billing, health |
| `src/db/models/` | 3 | Zod schemas for User, Team, Invitation |
| `src/db/queries/` | 3 | Supabase CRUD operations per entity |
| `src/services/` | 4 | Business logic: auth, teams, billing, email |
| `src/lib/` | 4 | Env validation, error classes, Supabase client factories |
| `tests/` | 7 | Unit tests for auth, API middleware, models, errors |

**Naming conventions:** Files use kebab-case. Exports are named (no default exports). Validation schemas share names with their TypeScript types via Zod's `z.infer`.
sections.summary: 44 files across 8 modules: auth, middleware, routes, models, queries, services, lib, tests

evidence.rationale: Representative route handler showing naming and import conventions

sections.title: Complexity & Risk Areas
sections.content:
**High complexity:**
- `src/services/billing-service.ts` — Stripe integration with checkout, webhook handling, subscription lifecycle, and plan transitions. Webhook processing must be idempotent. The `handleWebhook` function processes three event types; missing events (e.g. `invoice.paid`) could cause state drift.
- `src/services/team-service.ts` — Permission checks are inline in each function rather than centralized. Adding new roles or permission changes requires updating multiple functions.

**Security considerations:**
- JWT secret in env var (`JWT_SECRET`) — must be rotated carefully since active sessions will invalidate
- Magic link tokens are single-use and time-limited (15 minutes) — good practice
- Rate limiter is in-memory and resets on server restart — not suitable for multi-instance deployments
- Session cookies use `httpOnly`, `secure` (in production), `sameSite: lax` — solid defaults

**Potential issues:**
- No transaction boundaries around team creation + membership insertion — partial failure could leave orphaned teams
- Usage tracking increment is atomic but the check-then-increment in `trackUsage()` has a TOCTOU race under concurrent requests
sections.summary: Billing webhook complexity, inline permission checks, in-memory rate limiter not multi-instance safe, TOCTOU in usage tracking

evidence.rationale: Webhook handler with multiple event types and state transitions

sections.title: Suggested Starting Points
sections.content:
**To understand the request lifecycle:** Start at `src/middleware.ts` (edge middleware) → follow into `src/api/middleware/auth.ts` (JWT extraction) → then any route handler in `src/api/routes/`.

**To understand the data model:** Read `src/db/models/user.ts`, `team.ts`, and `invitation.ts` in that order — they define all entities and validation schemas.

**To understand the auth system:** Start at `src/auth/jwt.ts` (token signing) → `src/auth/session.ts` (cookie management) → `src/services/auth-service.ts` (orchestration).

**To understand billing:** Read `src/db/models/team.ts` for plan definitions → `src/services/billing-service.ts` for Stripe integration → `src/api/routes/billing.ts` for the API surface.

**To run tests:** Execute `npm test` — tests cover JWT operations, password hashing, rate limiting, request validation, data model schemas, and error handling.
sections.summary: Start with middleware.ts for request flow, db/models/ for data model, auth/ for authentication, billing-service.ts for payments

evidence.rationale: Entry point for understanding the request lifecycle
